bins.name: Optional joint load-generation study
bins.desc: Tract supports studying load and co-located generation together at the same point of interconnection. It insists this be at both parties' option and designed to produce faster or cheaper outcomes, not imposed as a requirement.
quotes: Tract supports DOE's proposal to study load and hybrid facilities together with generating facilities located at the same point of interconnection. Such joint study process should be at the option of both the load and generation customer and should be designed in such a way to result in faster and/or less costly interconnection for both the load and the generator.

bins.name: Cost-causation deposits and security
bins.desc: Tract accepts standardized deposits and financial security in principle but insists they be sized to actual incremental costs, not set as revenue guarantees for utilities. Security should be posted at project milestones rather than as an upfront lump sum, and agreements must be assignable to creditworthy third parties.
quotes: Tract agrees with adopting standardized study deposits, readiness requirements, and withdrawal penalties, provided that they are consistent with the Commission's cost-causation principles.

Adopting deposits or penalties that are not sized to reflect a project's impact on the system will unnecessarily and economically deter data center development, threatening the ability to achieve the Administration's dual goals of energy dominance and winning the AI race.

The Commission should establish a standardized methodology, whether by requiring fixed deposit amounts based on load size, by kWh, or through another metric, whereby customers can reasonably calculate anticipated charges and plan their investments accordingly.

Tract appreciates the need to provide financial security for load interconnection requests, but believes financial security requirements should be clear, predictable, in line with cost-causation, and tied to project progress.

Consistent with the Commission's cost-causation principles, financial security should only be required for actual incremental costs caused by the large load customer, and should not provide a windfall to transmission providers in the event of default.

Tract recommends the Commission require large load interconnection customers to only post financial security as they approach key project milestones (i.e., to support detailed design, permitting and right-of-way acquisition, procurement, pad acceptance, construction and energization), rather than up front as a lump sum because this places an unnecessary financial burden on the large load interconnection customer and creates incremental impediments to development.
The Commission should require that large load interconnection agreements and any related financial security agreements be assignable to creditworthy third parties.

bins.name: Voluntary net-study for hybrid facilities
bins.desc: Tract agrees hybrid facilities should have the option to elect a netted interconnection study but opposes making it mandatory, noting that some hybrids need full injection or withdrawal rights beyond what netting would allow.
quotes: Tract agrees that load and generation interconnection requests should have the option to undertake netted study situations, but it should not be required.

bins.name: Expedited study for curtailable loads
bins.desc: Tract endorses fast-tracking interconnection studies for loads that commit to curtailability or dispatchability, suggesting a 60-day study target using cluster or serialized queue processes.
quotes: Tract agrees with the proposal to expedite the interconnection study of large loads that agree to be curtailable and hybrid facilities that agree to be curtailable and dispatchable.

bins.name: Credit mechanism for shared upgrade costs
bins.desc: Tract supports crediting load customers for network upgrade costs when other customers use those upgrades, with credits continuing until 100% cost recovery rather than ending after a fixed period.
quotes: To the extent any network upgrades are assigned through interconnection studies to load or hybrid facilities, such costs should be offset through a crediting mechanism based on the actual usage of those upgrades by other networked customers.

bins.name: Standardized study rules and deadlines
bins.desc: Tract urges FERC to apply Order No. 2023-style mandatory study deadlines and queue management rules to large load interconnection, including disclosure of timelines, process steps, and how competing requests are handled.
quotes: Tract supports DOE's proposal to study load and hybrid facilities together with generating facilities located at the same point of interconnection. Such joint study process should be at the option of both the load and generation customer and should be designed in such a way to result in faster and/or less costly interconnection for both the load and the generator.
The Commission should apply the lessons it learned in the generator interconnection process and adopt similar deadlines for studying standardized load interconnection requests.

the Commission should establish standardized rules for each step in the large load interconnection process, the expected timing of each step, and other guidelines governing queue management, including whether the transmission provider is employing a cluster study or sequential study model, similar to generator interconnection.
Tract agrees with the proposal to expedite the interconnection study of large loads that agree to be curtailable and hybrid facilities that agree to be curtailable and dispatchable.

Tract agrees that there must be a plan to implement the proposed reforms that recognize the time, capital, and effort spent on requests that are already being studied for interconnection. Negatively affecting those existing requests not only wastes individual requestor's time, capital, and effort, but could also negatively impact all large load requests by causing cascading re-studies that delay or increase the costs of other large load requests.

bins.name: Cost-causation financial requirements
bins.desc: Tract consistently argues that all financial requirements, deposits, security amounts, and upgrade cost assignments must follow cost-causation principles and align with the generator interconnection precedent. Over-sized charges will deter investment in data center development.
quotes: Tract agrees with adopting standardized study deposits, readiness requirements, and withdrawal penalties, provided that they are consistent with the Commission's cost-causation principles.

Adopting deposits or penalties that are not sized to reflect a project's impact on the system will unnecessarily and economically deter data center development, threatening the ability to achieve the Administration's dual goals of energy dominance and winning the AI race.

The Commission should establish a standardized methodology, whether by requiring fixed deposit amounts based on load size, by kWh, or through another metric, whereby customers can reasonably calculate anticipated charges and plan their investments accordingly.

Tract appreciates the need to provide financial security for load interconnection requests, but believes financial security requirements should be clear, predictable, in line with cost-causation, and tied to project progress.

Consistent with the Commission's cost-causation principles, financial security should only be required for actual incremental costs caused by the large load customer, and should not provide a windfall to transmission providers in the event of default.

Tract recommends the Commission require large load interconnection customers to only post financial security as they approach key project milestones (i.e., to support detailed design, permitting and right-of-way acquisition, procurement, pad acceptance, construction and energization), rather than up front as a lump sum because this places an unnecessary financial burden on the large load interconnection customer and creates incremental impediments to development.

To the extent any network upgrades are assigned through interconnection studies to load or hybrid facilities, such costs should be offset through a crediting mechanism based on the actual usage of those upgrades by other networked customers.

bins.name: Optional co-located generation study
bins.desc: Tract supports optional joint study of co-located load and generation but opposes requiring it, arguing that flexibility in hybrid study elections is needed to accommodate diverse commercial arrangements.
quotes: Tract supports DOE's proposal to study load and hybrid facilities together with generating facilities located at the same point of interconnection. Such joint study process should be at the option of both the load and generation customer and should be designed in such a way to result in faster and/or less costly interconnection for both the load and the generator.
Tract agrees that load and generation interconnection requests should have the option to undertake netted study situations, but it should not be required.

bins.name: Flexibility incentives for curtailable loads
bins.desc: Tract backs expedited study for loads offering curtailability or dispatchability as a tool to reduce system impact and speed interconnection.
quotes: Tract agrees with the proposal to expedite the interconnection study of large loads that agree to be curtailable and hybrid facilities that agree to be curtailable and dispatchable.

bins.name: Usage-based upgrade cost credits
bins.desc: Tract supports crediting load customers for network upgrade costs proportional to actual use by other networked customers, with no fixed expiration on the credit period.
quotes: To the extent any network upgrades are assigned through interconnection studies to load or hybrid facilities, such costs should be offset through a crediting mechanism based on the actual usage of those upgrades by other networked customers.

bins.name: Even playing field for all developers
bins.desc: Tract stresses that interconnection rules must not create barriers or discriminate based on developer size or business model, given that non-hyperscale operators own most U.S. data center capacity today and will continue to do so.
quotes: The rules that emerge from this proceeding should ensure that developers continue to be able to compete for load interconnection capacity without confronting unduly burdensome barriers to entry.
any rules adopted by the Commission should provide an even playing field for all data center developers, without unduly discriminating against companies on the basis of their business model or size.

bins.name: Predictable cost methodology for planning
bins.desc: Tract argues that a standardized, transparent cost methodology is necessary so large load customers can forecast charges and make informed investment decisions, reducing stranded-investment risk for all parties.
quotes: The Commission should establish a standardized methodology, whether by requiring fixed deposit amounts based on load size, by kWh, or through another metric, whereby customers can reasonably calculate anticipated charges and plan their investments accordingly.

Tract appreciates the need to provide financial security for load interconnection requests, but believes financial security requirements should be clear, predictable, in line with cost-causation, and tied to project progress.

bins.name: Transparent queue management rules
bins.desc: Tract calls for standardized queue rules that disclose timelines, process steps, and how multiple competing requests are managed, mirroring the cluster and sequential study options in generator interconnection.
quotes: the Commission should establish standardized rules for each step in the large load interconnection process, the expected timing of each step, and other guidelines governing queue management, including whether the transmission provider is employing a cluster study or sequential study model, similar to generator interconnection.

bins.name: Milestone-based security posting
bins.desc: Tract recommends that financial security be posted incrementally at key project milestones rather than as an upfront lump sum, matching each dollar of security to each dollar the transmission provider commits, no more and no earlier.
quotes: Tract recommends the Commission require large load interconnection customers to only post financial security as they approach key project milestones (i.e., to support detailed design, permitting and right-of-way acquisition, procurement, pad acceptance, construction and energization), rather than up front as a lump sum because this places an unnecessary financial burden on the large load interconnection customer and creates incremental impediments to development.

bins.name: Assignability of interconnection agreements
bins.desc: Tract recommends that interconnection and financial security agreements be assignable by right to creditworthy third parties, affiliates, or successors to support project financing, continuity, and a competitive development ecosystem.
quotes: The Commission should require that large load interconnection agreements and any related financial security agreements be assignable to creditworthy third parties.

bins.name: Protect in-flight requests during transition
bins.desc: Tract supports a transition plan that gives existing interconnection applicants the option to benefit from new rules without being required to re-study or bear added costs, avoiding cascading delays.
quotes: Tract agrees that there must be a plan to implement the proposed reforms that recognize the time, capital, and effort spent on requests that are already being studied for interconnection. Negatively affecting those existing requests not only wastes individual requestor's time, capital, and effort, but could also negatively impact all large load requests by causing cascading re-studies that delay or increase the costs of other large load requests.
